compile.output: No compiler errors or warnings. Compiled with -O3 -march=native -std=c++17 -Wall -lpthread.

run.output: Query executed successfully. Returned 10 rows. Wall-clock time: 2.236s (program reported 1898ms internal).

validation.comparison_result: 10 rows returned, all match ground truth exactly. Columns: l_orderkey, revenue (sum of l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount)), o_orderdate, o_shippriority. Example: (4791171, 440715.2185, 1995-02-23, 0).

profiling.note: perf not used. Based on code analysis: high memory traffic, lock contention, inefficient join nesting.

Q3.notes: Correct result set. Performance is slow due to multiple concurrent bottlenecks: undersized thread pool (capped at 32 vs 64 cores), lock contention on map merges, nested loop joins, no SIMD, no zone map filtering.

Q3.bottleneck: Multi-level performance issues: (1) thread pool undersized to 32 threads when 64 cores available; (2) lock-guard merges in hot loop kill parallelism; (3) join strategy uses nested loops over OrderRecords per lineitem; (4) no SIMD for filtering/arithmetic; (5) zone maps not used for date range skipping; (6) std::unordered_map has poor collision performance on hash aggregation.
Q3.root_cause: Generated code prioritizes correctness and basic parallelism but misses critical optimizations: insufficient thread saturation, synchronization overhead, suboptimal join algorithms, missing hardware-specific optimizations (SIMD, cache awareness). Pipeline: Customer filter → hash join w/ orders → hash join w/ lineitem + aggregation. Each stage uses per-thread intermediate results merged with locks, creating contention. Lineitem (60M rows) is full-scanned with no block-level filtering despite available zone maps.

analysis.overall: Q3 produces correct results (top-10 orders by revenue) but at 1898ms is 20-25x slower than DuckDB (avg 91ms) and 5x slower than GenDB baseline (386ms). The query is dominated by full-table lineitem scan (60M rows), multi-way hash joins (customer→orders→lineitem), and aggregation into ~2M groups. With 64-core hardware and HDD storage, the implementation needs: (1) higher thread count (40-48 cores vs 32), (2) lock-free merge or thread-local-only aggregation, (3) better join ordering/strategy, (4) SIMD vectorization on filtering, (5) zone map utilization for block skipping, and (6) more aggressive predicate pushdown.

critical_fixes.issue: Results are correct but performance is critically poor (1898ms vs target ~90-400ms)
critical_fixes.fix: Not a semantic/correctness issue. This is a performance optimization task. No critical_fixes needed as the output is correct.
critical_fixes.description: All critical_fixes entries are NON-OPTIONAL; they address crashes, wrong results, or OOM. Since Q3 produces correct output, critical_fixes is empty.

performance_optimizations.technique: Increase thread pool from 32 to 48-56 threads
performance_optimizations.description: Current code at line 125 uses `std::min(32, (int)std::thread::hardware_concurrency())`. With 64 cores, this caps at 32. Change to `std::min(56, (int)std::thread::hardware_concurrency())` to saturate more cores. Expected gain: ~10-15% throughput on CPU-bound filtering/join/aggregation phases.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~200ms reduction (10-15% of 1898ms). Reason: with 64 cores available, 32 threads leave 32 cores idle. Increasing to 48-56 threads enables more parallelism on the customer filter (1.5M rows), orders join (15M rows), and lineitem scan (60M rows).

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_map with faster hash table (Abseil or open-addressing)
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses std::unordered_map for customer_custkey_map, orders_map, and local_aggregates. std::unordered_map uses chaining (linked lists), which has poor cache locality. Replace with: (a) Abseil FlatHashMap (2-5x faster, SIMD-accelerated lookups), OR (b) custom open-addressing hash table with linear probing. At line 172 (customer_custkey_map), 215 (orders_map), 262 (local_aggregates).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~300-400ms reduction (15-25% of 1898ms). Reason: Hash operations on millions of entries dominate. Abseil's SIMD probing and better cache locality reduce collision chains and L1 misses. Local_aggregates especially benefits since aggregation is the final hot loop.

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize join order: build small customer→orders hash table FIRST, then probe lineitem ONCE
performance_optimizations.description: Current strategy: (1) filter customer, (2) join orders with customer, (3) join lineitem with orders result. Problem: stores all OrderRecords per orderkey in orders_map; then loops over them in lineitem processing (line 278). Better approach: (1) filter customer (~323K rows), (2) hash-join with orders on custkey, producing filtered orders set (~2.2M rows). (3) hash-join with lineitem on orderkey (single join, not nested loops). This reduces redundant iterations when a single orderkey has multiple lineitems. Rewrite steps 1-3 to use partition-based join or simpler two-way joins without storing intermediate vectors.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~200-300ms reduction (10-20% of 1898ms). Reason: Avoids nested iteration over OrderRecords in the lineitem loop (line 278). Current design has implicit O(n_lineitem * avg_records_per_order) behavior. With better join sequencing, becomes O(n_lineitem) with hash lookups.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use zone maps on o_orderdate and l_shipdate to skip blocks
performance_optimizations.description: Storage design provides zone maps (line 31-33 in storage_design.json): l_shipdate_sorted, o_orderdate_sorted. Current code does NOT use them; it scans all rows of lineitem (60M) and orders (15M). With zone maps on sorted data, can skip blocks with dates outside [1992-01-01, 1995-03-15) for orders and [1995-03-16, 1998-12-31] for lineitem. Implement: (a) Load zone map metadata from storage. (b) Before processing each morsel in step 2 (orders), check if block's date range intersects filter. (c) Similarly for lineitem in step 3. Expected selectivity: ~15% for orders (o_orderdate < 1995-03-15), ~33% for lineitem (l_shipdate > 1995-03-15), so skip 85% and 67% respectively.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~300-500ms reduction (15-25% of 1898ms). Reason: Zone maps enable block-level skipping. Orders: 15M * 0.85 = 12.75M rows skipped. Lineitem: 60M * 0.67 = 40.2M rows skipped. However, requires implementing zone map metadata loading and block-aware iteration (significant refactoring). Conservative estimate: 15-25% since I/O overhead is lower on modern SSDs.

performance_optimizations.technique: Add SIMD vectorization for filtering and arithmetic
performance_optimizations.description: With AVX-512 support (line 7 in workload_analysis.json), vectorize: (a) date comparisons (o_orderdate < cutoff) on 8 int32_t values per iteration using AVX-512. (b) Revenue calculation (l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount)) on 4 doubles per iteration. (c) Dictionary lookup for c_mktsegment == building_code. Use _mm512 intrinsics or auto-vectorization pragmas. Affects lines 185 (customer filter), 229 (orders date filter), 276 (revenue calc).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~150-250ms reduction (8-15% of 1898ms). Reason: SIMD processes 4-8 values per cycle vs 1 scalar. Filtering dominates on large tables; 4-8x throughput on filters = ~10-15% overall speedup (filtering is ~20-30% of time). Conservative since memory bandwidth and ILP limit gains.

performance_optimizations.technique: Avoid lock contention by using purely thread-local aggregation (no global merge lock)
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses mutex at lines 192 and 240 to merge thread-local results into global maps. This serializes merges, killing parallelism when one thread finishes first. Better approach: (a) use per-thread local_aggregates without any global intermediate map for customer/orders phases, OR (b) use atomic CAS-based merge for the final aggregation merge (line 297) instead of mutex. For customer and orders filters, thread-local results can be kept separate and iterated directly. This moves synchronization overhead off the critical path.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~100-200ms reduction (5-10% of 1898ms). Reason: Lock contention is not the primary bottleneck (only 2 merge points per stage) but serializes briefly. Removing locks saves ~100-200ms on 1898ms baseline, assuming 5-10% of time is on merges.

summary: Q3 iteration 1 produces CORRECT results (10 rows matching ground truth) but executes in 1898ms, which is 20-25x slower than DuckDB (91ms avg) and 5x slower than GenDB baseline (386ms). The query involves 60M-row lineitem scan with multi-way hash joins (customer, orders) and aggregation into ~2M groups. Root causes: (1) thread pool capped at 32 threads vs 64 available cores; (2) lock contention on merges; (3) inefficient join strategy with nested loops; (4) no SIMD; (5) zone maps not utilized. Recommended priority sequence: (P1) increase thread count to 48-56, (P2) replace std::unordered_map with Abseil FlatHashMap, (P3) optimize join ordering, (P4) implement zone map filtering, (P5) add SIMD, (P6) reduce lock contention. Estimated gains: P1+P2+P3 = ~700-1100ms reduction, bringing performance to ~800-1200ms range. Further optimization (P4-P6) could approach 400-600ms.
